senses.definition: a person or thing that elicits fear or annoyance; a nervous or irritating person (informal).
examples: That salesman is a real skeeter, always pressuring you to buy.
The mosquitoes gave us trouble, but the term skeeter here refers to the insect, not the person.

senses.definition: a mosquito or fly; a small flying insect that bites or sucks blood (colloquial in American English).
examples: The skeeters were buzzing around the campsite all night.
We wore repellent to keep away the skeeters.

senses.definition: plural form referring to mosquitoes or biting insects; colloquial for skeeter.
examples: We had to flee indoors as the skeeters swarmed.
The skeeters are especially bad at dusk.

senses.definition: informal term for a small amount of drug use; can also refer to a quick, casual action (slang).
examples: He used to throw skeets of slang slang around.
In some contexts, skeets can mean quick, small sips or puffs.

senses.definition: more disreputable or repulsive in behavior or appearance; creepier.
examples: That late-night club had skeevier clientele than the daytime venue.
The basement room looked skeevier after the lights flickered.

senses.definition: the most disreputable or repulsive; extremely creepy.
examples: That back alley is skeeviest at night.
He told the skeeviest joke in the room.

senses.definition: disreputable or dirty; causing suspicion or discomfort.
examples: That skeevy bar needs a good cleaning.
His skeevy behavior made others wary.

senses.definition: invented surname used in pop culture (e.g., a character name); refers to a fictional or stylized surname.
examples: Skeezicks is the quirky surname used in the comic strip.
The family name in the show is Skeezicks.

senses.definition: a fictional name; originally a character in the comic strip 'Gasoline Alley'; used as a nickname for a boy or man in some contexts.
examples: Skeezix was the name of the strip’s young protagonist.
People sometimes call him Skeezix as a playful nickname.

senses.definition: a type of naval term referring to a small projection or fitting on a ship; obsolete or technical usage.
examples: The old vessel had a worn skef along the deck hatch.
Skef fittings are not common on modern ships.

senses.definition: a projections at the bottom of a boat that helps with stability and direction in the water; a fixed fin.
examples: The skeg keeps the boat tracking straight in windy conditions.
He replaced the damaged skeg on the kayak.

senses.definition: archaic or dialect term for beard or whiskers; also a surname in rare use.
examples: He shaved off his skegg before the portrait.
The term skegg appears in older texts.

senses.definition: a person who installs or works with skegs; a worker or craftsman in boatbuilding contexts; informal for someone who is large or bothersome in slang.
examples: The skegger repaired the damaged skeg on the boat.
In slang, you might hear, 'Don't be a skegger' meaning don't be a nuisance.

senses.definition: plural form referring to people who work with skegs or who act as skeggers; or as a playful nickname.
examples: The skeggers gathered to discuss maintenance plans.
Two skeggers joined the crew at dawn.

senses.definition: plural of skegg; can refer to multiple beards in archaic usage or as a surname.
examples: Old records mention several men with prominent skeggs.
The surname Skeggs appears in historical documents.

senses.definition: plural of skeg; the projecting fin on a boat or the extension used for stability.
examples: The skegs on the canoe were damaged in the collision.
Check the skegs before setting sail.

senses.definition: obsolete or dialect term with uncertain modern usage; may appear in historical texts.
examples: The term skeich appears in older dictionaries.
In some dialect writings, skeich is noted as a variant.

senses.definition: archaic or dialect term; may refer to a notch, gap, or a feather in some contexts; largely obsolete.
examples: The old chest bore a skeif mark.
Skeif appears in some rural dialect dictionaries.

senses.definition: obsolete or dialect term; could refer to a type of horse movement or a notch in wood, depending on region.
examples: In regional dialects, skeigh might describe a gait of a horse.
Some old texts use skeigh in woodworking contexts.

senses.definition: one who uses or studies skeigh or related terms; obscure usage.
examples: A skeigher might study regional dialects.
The compiler lists skeigher as a rare term.

senses.definition: superlative form of skeigh; extremely uncommon or archaic in modern usage.
examples: The skeighest dialect features in the anthology.
Skeighest usage appears only in niche texts.

senses.definition: somewhat skeigh; resembling skeigh terms in scholarship or dialect; marginally related.
examples: The skeighish notes in the appendix show regional variation.
Her speech had a skeighish flavor.

senses.definition: shifty, suspicious, or sly in manner; used informally.
examples: He gave a skeily look and slunk away.
That skeily behavior made us cautious.

senses.definition: a length of yarn wound in a loose coil; also a tangled mess of lines or threads.
examples: She bought a skein of cotton yarn for knitting.
A skein of thoughts ran through his mind.

senses.definition: past tense of skein; to wind into a skein; to form into a tangled coil.
examples: She skeined the yarn for steaming.
The yarn was skeined into neat lengths.

senses.definition: one who skeins; a person who winds yarn into skeins; a device used to wind yarn.
examples: The skeiner sped up the process of preparing yarn.
A mechanical skeiner helps wind yarn evenly.

senses.definition: past participle of skein; wound into a skein.
examples: The thread was skeined and ready for dyeing.
She had skeined several skeins of wool.

senses.definition: one who skeins; a person or machine that winds yarn into skeins.
examples: The skeiner attaches to the loom to prepare threads.
Industrial skeiners speed up fabric production.
